questions.question:
A. Translate the arithmetic expression a^n = (b + c) into a syntax tree.

B. A grammar is said to have cycles if it is the case that A =>+ A for some non-terminal A (where =>+ means 'derives in one or more steps'). Show that no grammar that has cycles can be LL(k) for any k.
questions.answer: See solution: (A) Draw the syntax tree with '=' as root, left child is a^n (power node with 'a' and 'n'), right child is '+' node with 'b' and 'c'. (B) A grammar with a cycle A =>+ A cannot be LL(k) because it leads to infinite loops during LL parsing — the parser cannot make a deterministic choice after any k tokens of lookahead.
questions.to_find: Part A: syntax tree for a^n = (b+c). Part B: proof that a grammar with cycles cannot be LL(k).

understand.plain: Part A asks you to draw the parse/syntax tree for the expression a^n = (b+c), where '^' is the exponentiation operator. Part B asks you to show that if a grammar has a cycle (a non-terminal that can derive itself in one or more steps), it cannot be an LL(k) grammar for any fixed k, because LL parsers require deterministic parsing decisions.

keywords.explain: A tree representation of an expression where interior nodes are operators and leaves are operands.
keywords.example: For a+b, the tree has '+' at the root with 'a' and 'b' as children.

keywords.explain: A grammar that can be parsed left-to-right with leftmost derivation using k tokens of lookahead deterministically.
keywords.example: LL(1) means one token of lookahead is sufficient to choose the correct production.

keywords.explain: A situation where a non-terminal A can derive itself: A =>+ A in one or more derivation steps.
keywords.example: If A -> B and B -> A, then A =>+ A is a cycle.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='340' height='240'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='140' y='10' width='50' height='36' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='165' y='33' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>=</text><line x1='165' y1='46' x2='80' y2='90' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='165' y1='46' x2='250' y2='90' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='55' y='90' width='50' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='113' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>^</text><rect x='225' y='90' width='50' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='113' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>+</text><line x1='80' y1='126' x2='45' y2='170' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='126' x2='115' y2='170' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='250' y1='126' x2='215' y2='170' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='250' y1='126' x2='285' y2='170' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='20' y='170' width='50' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='45' y='193' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a</text><rect x='90' y='170' width='50' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='115' y='193' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>n</text><rect x='190' y='170' width='50' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='215' y='193' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>b</text><rect x='260' y='170' width='50' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='285' y='193' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>c</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Syntax tree for a^n = (b+c): root '=' node, left subtree is '^' with children 'a' and 'n', right subtree is '+' with children 'b' and 'c'.

given.aim: Part A: Draw syntax tree for a^n = (b+c). Part B: Prove no grammar with a cycle A =>+ A can be LL(k).

terms.meaning: An arithmetic expression with assignment/equality, exponentiation, and addition.
terms.example: a^2 = (3+5) is an instance of this pattern.
terms.connects: The expression structure determines the shape of the syntax tree.

terms.meaning: A non-terminal A that can derive itself in one or more steps.
terms.example: A -> B, B -> A creates a cycle: A => B => A.
terms.connects: A cycle means the parser can loop forever trying to match A.

terms.meaning: Top-down deterministic parsing using k lookahead tokens to select productions.
terms.example: LL(1) parsers use one token to decide which rule to apply.
terms.connects: LL(k) requires that for any non-terminal, the correct production is uniquely determined by k tokens of lookahead.

given.plan: For Part A, build the tree bottom-up following operator precedence: '^' binds tightest, then '+', then '='. For Part B, assume a grammar G has cycle A =>+ A; show that an LL(k) parser would enter an infinite loop, contradicting the termination requirement of LL parsing.

steps.title: Build syntax tree for a^n = (b+c)
steps.apply: The top-level operator is '='. Left operand is a^n (exponentiation): '^' node with children 'a' (left) and 'n' (right). Right operand is (b+c): '+' node with children 'b' and 'c'. Root is '=' with left child '^' subtree and right child '+' subtree.
steps.note: In a syntax tree, the operator with lowest precedence appears closest to the root.

steps.title: Prove grammar with cycles cannot be LL(k)
steps.apply: Suppose grammar G has a cycle: A =>* A via some sequence of derivation steps. In LL(k) parsing, the parser must choose a production for A by looking at k tokens of lookahead. When production A -> alpha is chosen and alpha =>* A (cycle), the parser is back to expanding A with the same k lookahead tokens, leading to an infinite derivation that never consumes input. For a grammar to be LL(k), every sentential form derived must be parseable in finite steps. A cycle means the parser can repeatedly apply productions without consuming any input, making it impossible to guarantee termination or deterministic parsing. Therefore, no grammar with cycles can be LL(k) for any k.
steps.note: Cycles also violate the requirement that LL(k) grammars be non-left-recursive in a broader sense — any grammar that can loop without consuming input is not suitable for LL parsing.

solution.result: Part A: The syntax tree has '=' as root; left subtree is '^' with children 'a' and 'n'; right subtree is '+' with children 'b' and 'c'. Part B: A grammar with a cycle A =>+ A cannot be LL(k) because the parser would loop infinitely, contradicting the finite termination and determinism requirements of LL(k) parsing.

questions.formula_note: LL(k) parsing requires deterministic, terminating top-down parsing; cycles cause infinite loops making LL(k) parsing impossible.

questions.question:
Let G be a context-free grammar where G = ({S, A, B, C}, {a, b, d}, P, S) with the productions P given below (epsilon denotes the null/empty string):

S -> AbB | c
A -> aAb | epsilon
B -> Ad | b
C -> epsilon

Transform the grammar G to an equivalent context-free grammar G' that has no epsilon-productions and no unit productions. (A unit production is of the form X -> Y where X and Y are non-terminals.)
questions.answer:
Equivalent grammar G' with no epsilon-productions and no unit productions:
S -> AbB | aAbB | AbAd | aAbAd | bB | Ab | b | bAd | c | Bb | bd | ad | aAbd | Abd
A -> aAb | ab
B -> Ad | aAbd | d | b | abd
(Detailed elimination is shown in the solution steps.)
questions.to_find: Equivalent CFG with no epsilon-productions and no unit productions.

understand.plain: You are given a context-free grammar with some productions that produce the empty string (epsilon-productions) and possibly unit productions (a non-terminal producing just another non-terminal). The task is to transform this grammar into an equivalent one — generating the same language — without those two types of productions.

keywords.explain: A grammar rule of the form A -> epsilon, meaning the non-terminal A can produce the empty string.
keywords.example: A -> epsilon means A can disappear (be replaced by nothing).

keywords.explain: A grammar rule of the form A -> B where both A and B are non-terminals (right side is a single non-terminal).
keywords.example: S -> A is a unit production.

keywords.explain: A non-terminal that can derive epsilon (the empty string) in zero or more steps.
keywords.example: If A -> epsilon, then A is nullable. If B -> A and A is nullable, then B is also nullable.

understand.visual_alt: CFG transformation: first find nullable non-terminals, then eliminate epsilon-productions by adding all combinations of including/excluding nullable symbols; then eliminate unit productions by substituting the right-hand side of unit rules.

given.aim: Remove all epsilon-productions and unit productions from G while preserving the language L(G).

terms.meaning: The set of non-terminals that can derive epsilon.
terms.example: In G, A -> epsilon so A is nullable. C -> epsilon so C is nullable.
terms.connects: Every nullable non-terminal's occurrences in rules must be optionally omitted when removing epsilon-productions.

terms.meaning: S -> AbB | c; A -> aAb | epsilon; B -> Ad | b; C -> epsilon
terms.example: A -> aAb means A derives a, then A (recursively), then b.
terms.connects: These are the starting rules; we transform them to eliminate epsilon and unit productions.

terms.meaning: A grammar G' that generates exactly the same language as G (possibly excluding epsilon if the original language did not include it).
terms.example: G' must accept all strings accepted by G, including all strings derivable via epsilon-rules, but not using epsilon-rules directly.
terms.connects: The goal is to replace epsilon-rules with explicit alternatives covering all possibilities.

given.plan: Step 1: Find all nullable non-terminals (those that can derive epsilon). Step 2: Eliminate epsilon-productions by adding new rules for each possible inclusion/exclusion of nullable symbols. Step 3: Find unit productions and eliminate them by replacing X -> Y with all rules reachable from Y.

steps.title: Find nullable non-terminals
steps.apply:
A -> epsilon => A is nullable.
C -> epsilon => C is nullable.
B -> Ad: A is nullable, so if A is removed we get B -> d (not epsilon). B -> b (terminal, not epsilon). So B is NOT nullable.
S -> AbB: even if A is removed, bB remains; not epsilon. S -> c (terminal). S is NOT nullable.
So Nullable = {A, C}. Note: C does not appear in any other production, so it has no effect on other rules.
steps.note: C is nullable but appears in no production body, so it affects nothing except itself.

steps.title: Eliminate epsilon-productions
steps.formula_raw: For each production with nullable symbols, add variants with those symbols present and absent.
steps.apply:
For A -> aAb: A appears on the right and A is nullable. Add: A -> aAb (keep A), A -> ab (remove A). Drop A -> epsilon.
For S -> AbB: A is nullable. Add: S -> AbB (keep A), S -> bB (remove A). B is not nullable so no further variants.
For B -> Ad: A is nullable. Add: B -> Ad (keep A), B -> d (remove A). 
For B -> b: no nullables, keep as is.
S -> c: keep as is.
New productions after epsilon-elimination:
S -> AbB | bB | c
A -> aAb | ab
B -> Ad | d | b
steps.note: We drop A -> epsilon and C -> epsilon. We keep S -> c since epsilon is not in L(G) (S can never derive epsilon with these rules).

steps.title: Check for and eliminate unit productions
steps.formula_raw: For X -> Y (unit production), replace with X -> alpha for all Y -> alpha in the current grammar.
steps.apply:
After step 2, the productions are:
S -> AbB | bB | c
A -> aAb | ab
B -> Ad | d | b
Check each production right-hand side:
- S -> AbB: right side has two symbols (A and bB), not a single non-terminal. Not a unit production.
- S -> bB: 'b' is a terminal followed by B, not a single non-terminal. Not a unit production.
- S -> c: 'c' is a terminal. Not a unit production.
- A -> aAb: not a unit production.
- A -> ab: not a unit production.
- B -> Ad: not a unit production.
- B -> d: 'd' is a terminal. Not a unit production.
- B -> b: 'b' is a terminal. Not a unit production.
There are no unit productions in the grammar after step 2. The grammar is already free of unit productions.
steps.note: It's important to check after epsilon-elimination, since that step can sometimes introduce or reveal unit productions.

steps.title: Final equivalent grammar G'
steps.apply:
G' = ({S, A, B}, {a, b, c, d}, P', S) where P' is:
S -> AbB | bB | c
A -> aAb | ab
B -> Ad | d | b
This grammar has no epsilon-productions and no unit productions and generates L(G).
steps.note: C was removed since it only had C -> epsilon and did not appear in any other rule, making it useless after epsilon elimination.

solution.result: G' = ({S, A, B}, {a, b, c, d}, P', S) with productions: S -> AbB | bB | c; A -> aAb | ab; B -> Ad | d | b. This grammar is equivalent to G with no epsilon-productions and no unit productions.

questions.formula_note: Epsilon-production elimination: find nullable non-terminals, generate all subsets of nullable occurrences in each rule; then check for and eliminate unit productions by substitution.

questions.question:
The grammar whose productions are

<stmt> -> if <expr> then <stmt> | if <expr> then <stmt> else <stmt> | <id> := <id>

is ambiguous because

A. the sentence 'if a then if b then c := d' has more than two parse trees
B. the left most and right most derivations of the sentence 'if a then if b then c := d' give rise to different parse trees
C. the sentence 'if a then if b then c := d else c := d' has more than one parse tree
D. the sentence 'if a then if b then c := d else c := d' has two parse trees
questions.answer: D. the sentence 'if a then if b then c := d else c := d' has two parse trees
questions.to_find: The correct reason why the given if-then-else grammar is ambiguous.

understand.plain: A grammar is ambiguous if some string in its language has more than one parse tree (or equivalently, more than one leftmost/rightmost derivation). The classic dangling-else ambiguity arises when an 'else' can be paired with either of two preceding 'if's. The question asks which statement correctly identifies why this grammar is ambiguous.

keywords.explain: A context-free grammar where at least one string has two or more distinct parse trees.
keywords.example: E -> E+E | E*E | id is ambiguous: 'id+id*id' has two parse trees.

keywords.explain: The classic ambiguity in if-then-else grammars where an 'else' can be matched with either a nearby or farther 'if'.
keywords.example: 'if a then if b then s1 else s2' — the else can bind to the outer or inner if.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='10' y='22' class='tag ink'>Parse Tree 1: else with inner if</text><rect x='200' y='30' width='60' height='30' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='230' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>stmt</text><line x1='230' y1='60' x2='120' y2='100' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='230' y1='60' x2='340' y2='100' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1.5'/><rect x='80' y='100' width='80' height='28' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='120' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>if a then...</text><rect x='290' y='100' width='100' height='28' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='340' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>if b then...else</text><text x='10' y='165' class='tag ink'>Parse Tree 2: else with outer if</text><rect x='200' y='175' width='60' height='30' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='230' y='195' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>stmt</text><line x1='230' y1='205' x2='120' y2='235' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='230' y1='205' x2='340' y2='235' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1.5'/><rect x='70' y='228' width='110' height='28' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='125' y='246' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>if a then if b then...</text><rect x='290' y='228' width='80' height='28' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='330' y='246' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>else c:=d</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two parse trees for 'if a then if b then c:=d else c:=d': in one, else pairs with inner if; in the other, else pairs with outer if.

given.aim: Identify the correct statement that explains why the if-then-else grammar is ambiguous.

terms.example: The grammar allows both if-then and if-then-else statements, leading to dangling else ambiguity.
terms.connects: When else appears, it can associate with either the nearest or farthest unmatched if.

terms.meaning: 'if a then if b then c := d else c := d' has an else that can bind to either if.
terms.example: Interpretation 1: if a then (if b then c:=d else c:=d). Interpretation 2: (if a then if b then c:=d) else c:=d.
terms.connects: These two interpretations yield exactly two different parse trees, proving ambiguity.

terms.meaning: 'if a then if b then c := d' has no else, so only one parse tree exists (the else-less if-then).
terms.example: Without an else, there is no dangling else problem and only one parse tree is possible.
terms.connects: Options A and B are incorrect because the sentence without else is not ambiguous.

given.plan: Analyze each option by trying to construct parse trees for the given sentences. For sentences without 'else', check if multiple parse trees exist. For sentences with 'else', check the dangling-else binding.

steps.title: Analyze options A and B: sentence 'if a then if b then c := d'
steps.apply:
This sentence has no 'else'. With the given grammar, the only way to derive it is:
stmt -> if a then stmt -> if a then (if b then c:=d)
There is only ONE parse tree for this sentence. Options A and B claim it has multiple parse trees or different leftmost/rightmost derivation trees — both are FALSE.
steps.note: Without an 'else', there is no dangling-else ambiguity. The sentence has a unique parse tree.

steps.title: Analyze option C: claims more than one parse tree (implying 3+)
steps.apply: The sentence 'if a then if b then c := d else c := d' does have ambiguity, but the claim in option C is 'more than one parse tree.' While technically true (there are exactly two), option C is less precise than option D which correctly says exactly two parse trees.
steps.note: Option C says 'more than one' — could mean 2, 3, or more. It is technically true but vague. Option D says exactly 'two parse trees,' which is precise and correct.

steps.title: Analyze option D: sentence 'if a then if b then c := d else c := d' has exactly two parse trees
steps.apply:
Parse Tree 1 (else binds to inner if):
stmt => if a then [if b then c:=d else c:=d]
Here 'else c:=d' is paired with 'if b'.

Parse Tree 2 (else binds to outer if):
stmt => if a then [if b then c:=d] else c:=d
Here 'else c:=d' is paired with 'if a'.

These are two distinct parse trees, confirming ambiguity. Option D correctly states exactly two parse trees exist for this sentence.
steps.note: This is the classic dangling-else ambiguity. Option D is the most precise and correct statement of why the grammar is ambiguous.

solution.result: D. the sentence 'if a then if b then c := d else c := d' has two parse trees.

questions.formula_note: Dangling-else ambiguity: in grammars with both if-then and if-then-else, an else can bind to more than one if, producing multiple parse trees for the same sentence.
